title: Roast chicken
subtitle: Buttered breast, lemon and thyme in the cavity
excerpt: A whole chicken roasted at high heat with butter under the skin and aromatics inside. Ready in just over an hour and good enough to eat on its own.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Roast fowl entries.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg. Roasted chicken and basting methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: Leaving cooked meat in a warm place before carving, allowing the muscle fibres to relax and the juices to redistribute so the meat stays moist when cut.
Spooning or brushing pan juices or fat over meat during roasting to keep the surface moist and encourage browning.

content.text: Roast chicken is the most forgiving of the Sunday joints. The whole bird takes about 80 minutes in a hot oven, needs no complicated preparation, and produces a pan full of golden juices for gravy.
Butter pushed under the breast skin keeps the white meat moist. A lemon and a few sprigs of thyme in the cavity perfume the bird from the inside without complicating the recipe.
What you need
A roasting tin that fits the bird with a couple of centimetres around it is ideal. Too large and the juices spread thin and burn; too small and the bird steams.
Method
Prepare the bird
Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Pat the chicken dry inside and out with kitchen paper.

Loosen the breast skin with your fingers, working from the neck end down. Push most of the softened butter under the skin and spread it flat. Rub the remaining butter over the outside of the bird. Season generously all over with salt and pepper.
Push the lemon halves, thyme sprigs, and crushed garlic into the cavity. Scatter the quartered onion across the base of the roasting tin. Place the chicken breast-side up on the onion.
Roast
Roast for 20 minutes per 500 g plus 20 minutes extra (about 80 minutes for a 1.5 kg bird).
After 45 minutes,
baste
the bird with the pan juices. If the juices are catching, add a splash of water to the tin.
The chicken is done when the probe reads 75 degrees C at the thickest part of the thigh, away from the bone. The juices run clear when you pierce the thigh at its deepest point.
Rest and carve
Transfer to a warm board and tent loosely with foil.
Rest
for 15 minutes before carving.
Carve by removing the legs first, then slicing the breast away from the carcass. The carcass makes an excellent stock.

items.cause: The bird was overcooked, or the butter did not reach under the skin.
items.fix: Push butter right under the breast skin before roasting. Check the internal temperature early; 75 degrees C at the thigh is the target.

items.symptom: The skin is pale rather than golden
items.cause: The oven was not hot enough, or the bird was too cold going in.
items.fix: Make sure the oven is fully preheated. Pat the skin dry before buttering.

items.symptom: The legs are undercooked even when the breast is done
items.cause: The bird was not at room temperature, or the legs were tucked too close to the body.
items.fix: Let the bird come to room temperature before roasting. Tie the legs loosely with kitchen twine if needed.

items.cause: The trivet vegetables dried out, or the tin was too large.
items.fix: Add a splash of water to the tin if the juices start to catch.

content.text: Variations

For a simple gravy, pour off most of the fat, set the tin on the hob over medium heat, and pour in a glass of white wine or chicken stock. Scrape up the brown bits and let it reduce for a few minutes. A spatchcocked bird (backbone removed, pressed flat) cooks in about 45 minutes and gives more golden skin.
Where this dish lives

Roast chicken is the most democratic of the Sunday roasts: affordable, quick, and universally liked. Its method has changed little since the nineteenth century, when butter and aromatics in the cavity were already the standard approach. The French call it poulet roti; the British call it Sunday lunch.
